title: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 7.5 — Documented information control: policy register approval trail
exclusions: Does NOT edit policies; detects policy register entries missing an approval record or past their review cadence.
evidence: Saved search 'iso_27001_7_5_policy_trail' (weekly) emits per policy: policy_id, version, approved_by, approved_at, days_to_next_review, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Clause 7.5 Documented information — approval trail + cadence adherence directly evidenced.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 7.5 (Documented information) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.54: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 7.5 — Documented information control: policy register approval trail.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Setting a policy's approval to now()-cadence_days+29d returns days_to_next_review=1 (near-expiry).
controlTest.negativeScenario: A policy with a recent approval and 200 days remaining is absent.

dataSources: `index=grc` sourcetype=policyhub:policy (change of state); policy cadence lookup `iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv`. (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
spl:
index=grc sourcetype=policyhub:policy action=approved earliest=-400d
| stats latest(_time) AS approved_at latest(version) AS version latest(approved_by) AS approved_by BY policy_id
| lookup iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv policy_id OUTPUT cadence_days owner
| eval days_to_next_review=cadence_days-round((now()-approved_at)/86400,0)
| where days_to_next_review<=30 OR isnull(approved_at)
| table policy_id version approved_by approved_at days_to_next_review owner
description: For every policy in the register, produce the last approval record and the time to the next review. Policies within 30 days of expiry or with no approval record are surfaced for action.
value: Prevents the common deficiency of policies that 'used to be' approved but whose approval trail has expired — a direct Clause 7.5 non-conformance.
implementation: (1) Route policyhub approval events into index=grc; (2) maintain iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv; (3) schedule UC weekly; (4) days_to_next_review<=30 opens a review task to policy owner; (5) quarterly CISO review. Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=policyhub:policy`.
visualization: Table of near-expiry policies, bar chart by owner, single value 'policies expired'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Assessment cycle alignment** — Control assessments following annual cycles may show brief non-current periods between assessment completion and report publication.

2. **Finding remediation within SLA** — Open findings within their documented remediation timeline are being addressed per policy, not neglected or overlooked.

3. **Framework version transitions** — Migration between control framework versions (ISO 27001:2013 to 2022) creates temporary mapping gaps during the documented transition period.

4. **Accepted residual risk items** — Findings formally accepted through the risk management governance process with documented compensating controls and management sign-off.

5. **Evidence collection cadence differences** — Controls with different evidence generation frequencies (real-time vs. quarterly) show varying currency in compliance dashboards.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `grc` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `7.5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 7.5 (Documented information) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.54: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 7.5 — Documented information control: policy register approval trail." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `grc` under `policyhub:policy`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Route policyhub approval events into index=grc. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 2:** maintain iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC weekly. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** days_to_next_review<=30 opens a review task to policy owner. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** quarterly CISO review. Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=policyhub:policy`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 7.5 — Documented information control: policy register approval trail," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the ISO/IEC 27001 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv` — the policy register codifying the approved configuration baseline. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`cadence_days owner`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `policy_id`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval policy_id = lower(policy_id)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 400 days.

Numeric threshold `days_to_next_review <= 30`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.54: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 7.5 — Documented information contr]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -400d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.54,reg=ISO/IEC_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=policyhub:policy earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `policy_id`, `approved_at`, `approved_by`, `version`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=policyhub:policy earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table policy_id, approved_at, approved_by, version, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup iso_27001_policy_cadence.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_to_next_review`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_to_next_review) as p50 perc90(days_to_next_review) as p90 perc99(days_to_next_review) as p99 max(days_to_next_review) as hi
```

The current threshold `30` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.54: ISO/IEC 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Near-Expiry Policies (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart By Owner (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Policies Expired' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO/IEC 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 7.5 — Documented information control: policy register approval trail. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
